text: The surgeon suspends the gallbladder and takes down the round ligament, mobilizing adhesions. A stitch on the gallbladder retracts the liver for dissection of the hepatoduodenal ligament. Sponges are placed to protect the liver capsule during mobilization. An ultrasound reveals dilated left-sided bile ducts and an associated mass. A formal oncologic portal and celiac lymphadenectomy is performed.

The surgeon uses fluorescence imaging to identify the common hepatic duct and the left and right branches. The robotic vessel sealer is employed to dissect the left portal vein and hepatic artery on the left side. Locking plastic clips are applied to the hepatic arterial branches to the left lateral and medial sectors, enhancing visibility of the portal bifurcation. The left branch of the portal vein is controlled using short silk ties and locking plastic clips.

The surgeon visually identifies the demarcation between the left and right hemilivers after obtaining inflow control on the left side. Using robotic monopolar scissors, the left hepatic duct is precisely dissected intraparenchymally. The duct is then encircled with silk ties and subsequently transected.

The surgeon identifies and controls the left hepatic vein by dissecting the superior aspect of the liver, taking down the falciform ligament, and opening the coronary ligament. The left hepatic vein is temporarily controlled using umbilical tape and locking plastic clips.

The surgeon uses a robotic bipolar vessel sealing device in the right hand and a bipolar fenestrated instrument in the left hand to perform parenchymal transection. Branches from the middle hepatic vein are dissected, clipped, and divided or controlled with suture ligatures. A monofilament 5-0 suture is used to manage back bleeding from hepatic vein branches. The caudate lobe of the liver is removed, along with additional parenchymal portions from the superior aspect of the liver.

The surgeon divides the left hepatic vein and completes the parenchymal transection in a cephalad direction using the robotic vessel sealing device. The caudate lobe is dissected off the vena cava, revealing an additional branch of the left hepatic vein coming off the middle hepatic vein, which is encircled, clipped, and divided using robotic instruments.

The surgeon completes the left hepatectomy without using stapling devices, relying on silk ties and locking plastic clips for parenchymal control. The specimen is placed into fabric specimen bags for removal. Hemostatic control is achieved using bipolar electrical saline electrocautery and hemostatic agents.
